title: What Is Wealth Management?
quick_definition: The business of managing the whole financial life of an individual or family — planning, investing, tax, retirement, and estate — on their behalf and in their interest, organized around the family's goals over a finite human lifetime rather than around beating a market benchmark.
explainer_covers: The simple model: a household has goals (retire comfortably, send kids to college, leave a legacy), a finite and shifting time horizon, a tax bill, and limited capacity to bear loss — and wealth management coordinates everything (not just the portfolio) toward those goals.

What makes it wealth management and not asset management: it is goal-relative, not benchmark-relative (G160 vs. ★ G157); it is comprehensive (investing is one piece of a plan that also covers tax, insurance, retirement, and estate — G159); it is finite-horizon and tax-paying (an individual dies, decumulates, and pays taxes, where an endowment is perpetual and tax-exempt); and it is relationship-driven (the advisor coaches a person through fear and greed — G161).

The spectrum of who is served: from mass-affluent households (served by advisors, platforms, and increasingly robo-advisors) through high-net-worth (HNW, served by private banks and full-service advisors) to ultra-high-net-worth (UHNW, served by family offices) — one continuum by complexity and assets.

What the advisor actually delivers: a financial plan, an asset allocation built from low-cost products, ongoing rebalancing and tax management, retirement-income and estate coordination, and — most valuable and least visible — behavioral coaching.

The size and centrality of the industry: the channel through which the products of every other module reach households — the retail demand side for the funds asset managers run, the IPOs and bonds banks underwrite, and (for the wealthy) the PE, credit, and hedge funds the alternatives world produces.
connects_to_raw: Z1.2 (the contrast with AM), Z1.3 (`G159` financial planning), `G160` goals-based investing, `G161` the advisor relationship, `★ G155` fiduciary duty, and across the aisle: AM Z1.1 (the institutional buy-side it mirrors), AM Z1.9 (where AM flags this very module), AM Z5.3 (the advisor channel as AM's distribution), `★ G138` index fund (the core product it sells). **GAP: a current source for industry size, the advice-channel landscape, and the mass-affluent/HNW/UHNW segmentation.**

gaps.note: a current source for industry size, the advice-channel landscape, and the mass-affluent/HNW/UHNW segmentation

title: The Individual vs. the Institution: Wealth Management vs. Asset Management
quick_definition: Wealth management and asset management are the two halves of the money-management world split by whose money and what objective — asset management runs institutions' (and pooled retail) money against a benchmark at scale; wealth management runs a family's money against that family's goals over a human life — the same products and analytics, opposite seats.
explainer_covers: The shared ground: both invest in (mostly) the same liquid securities, both use the same allocation and product toolkit (★ G143–★ G149), both are fiduciary disciplines, and wealth management is in large part the distribution channel that buys what asset managers build (AM Z5.3).

The divide, axis by axis: objective (a goal/liability for a specific family, G160, vs. a benchmark-relative return, ★ G157); horizon (finite and shifting, ending in death and bequest, vs. perpetual or rolling); taxes (an individual pays real taxes on every gain and dividend — G166 — where the index, the endowment, and the 401(k) do not, making tax the individual's defining problem); the balance sheet (a household's biggest early asset is human capital, G162, which no institution has); decumulation (an individual must eventually turn the portfolio into lifetime income, G169 — an institution rarely "spends down" to zero); and the relationship (a person needs coaching, G161, where an institution's board does not).

The unifying reframe, in Swensen's own words and books: Pioneering Portfolio Management is the institutional manual (the endowment model, ★ G154); Unconventional Success is its individual sibling — and its conclusion is that the endowment model does not transfer to individuals (who lack elite manager-selection resources), so the right answer for nearly all households is the opposite of the endowment's active-alternatives tilt: low-cost, broadly-diversified, equity-oriented, tax-aware indexing.

Where the line blurs: the wealthiest individuals (UHNW, family offices, G177) can access the endowment model's alternatives (★ G9, ★ G56), and the giant asset managers now build wealth-channel and direct-indexing products — so the two worlds increasingly converge at the top and through technology (Z5).
connects_to_raw: `G160` goals-based investing, `★ G157` relative return (the contrast), `★ G154` endowment model (the institutional seat), `G162` human capital, `G166` tax-aware investing, `G169` decumulation, `G161` the advisor relationship, **AM Z1.1 / AM Z1.2** (the asset-management side of this map), **AM Z2.11** (the endowment model this contrasts with), AM Z1.9 (AM's flag to WM). *This is the spine of the whole module — build it as a featured cross-module node, the AM↔WM counterpart to the HF↔AM buy-side spine.*

title: Financial Planning: The Whole-Life Lens
quick_definition: The comprehensive, ongoing process of organizing a household's entire financial life — cash flow, saving, debt, insurance, investments, taxes, retirement, and estate — into one coordinated plan aimed at the family's goals, of which managing the portfolio is only a single component.
explainer_covers: The scope that separates planning from investing: a financial plan covers cash-flow and budgeting, debt management, emergency reserves, insurance and risk protection (life, disability, liability), investment management, tax planning (G166), retirement planning (G169), and estate planning (G173) — the portfolio is one chapter, not the book.

The planning process (the CFP-style loop): establish the relationship and goals → gather data → analyze the current state → develop recommendations → implement → monitor and revise — a repeating cycle, not a one-time document.

Goals as the organizing principle (G160): every recommendation traces back to a specific, dated, prioritized goal; the plan's job is to make the trade-offs among goals explicit (save more now vs. retire later vs. leave a legacy) and fund them in priority order.

The investment policy statement, individual version: the household's written objectives, constraints, risk capacity (G163), and target allocation — the personal analogue of the institution's IPS (AM Z3.1) and policy portfolio (★ G144).

Why the plan, not the portfolio, is the product: the largest financial outcomes for most households come from planning decisions — how much to save, when to retire, how to insure, how to structure the estate, how to manage taxes — not from squeezing extra basis points out of security selection.
connects_to_raw: `G159` (this node), `G160` goals-based investing, `G163` risk capacity, `G166` tax-aware investing, `G169` retirement decumulation, `G173` estate planning, `G161` the advisor relationship, Z3.1 (the planning process as the front of the WM "deal" flow), AM Z3.1 (the institutional IPS — *the same document, individual seat*). **GAP: a CFP-curriculum or financial-planning-process text — Monroe and Berns cover pieces, but not the integrated professional planning standard.**

gaps.note: a CFP-curriculum or financial-planning-process text — Monroe and Berns cover pieces, but not the integrated professional planning standard

title: Client Segments & Channels
quick_definition: Wealth management serves a spectrum of households — from mass-affluent to ultra-high-net-worth — through a matching spectrum of channels, from low-cost robo-advisors and platforms up to bespoke single-family offices, with service intensity and customization rising with assets and complexity.
explainer_covers: The segments: mass-market / mass-affluent (modest to a few million in investable assets — served by advisors, broker-dealers, fund platforms, and robo-advisors); high-net-worth (HNW, roughly single-digit millions and up — full-service advisors and private banks); very-high and ultra-high-net-worth (VHNW/UHNW, tens of millions and up — private banks' top tier, multi-family and single-family offices).

The channels, low- to high-touch: the robo-advisor (G178, automated, cheapest, mass-market); the independent RIA / financial advisor (fee-based planning and investment management); the wirehouse / broker-dealer (the traditional brokerage channel, historically commission-driven); private banking (G176, the bank's bundled HNW service); the multi-family office and single-family office (G177, the UHNW pinnacle).

How needs change up the spectrum: mass-affluent clients need low-cost allocation, planning, and behavioral discipline; HNW clients add tax complexity, concentrated-stock and liquidity-event management, and estate planning; UHNW families add bespoke alternatives access (★ G9), multi-generational governance, philanthropy, and operating-business and direct-investment complexity.

The economics of service intensity: low-asset clients are served profitably only at scale and through technology (the robo and platform model); high-asset clients support the cost of a dedicated team — which is why the industry is barbelling into cheap digital advice at one end and high-touch family-office service at the other.

The liquidity-event pipeline: many wealth-management clients are created by an event — a company sale, an IPO (★ G102), an inheritance, a large equity-comp vesting — which is where concentrated-stock management and the whole planning apparatus first engage.
connects_to_raw: `G176` private banking, `G177` the family office, `G178` robo-advisor, `G161` the advisor relationship, `★ G9` "2 and 20" (the alternatives UHNW clients reach), `★ G102` the IPO process (the liquidity event that creates clients), Z2 (the channels as strategy branches), Z5.3 (distribution, from the AM side). **GAP: current segmentation data and the advice-channel competitive landscape.**

gaps.note: current segmentation data and the advice-channel competitive landscape

title: Human Capital & the Individual's Balance Sheet
quick_definition: The largest asset most people own early in life is not their portfolio but their human capital — the present value of their future earnings — and treating it as a (usually bond-like) balance-sheet asset that depletes over a working life is the key to sizing how much investment risk a person can actually take.
explainer_covers: The concept (Berns): human capital = the PV of future labor income. For a young worker it dwarfs financial capital; over a career it is steadily converted into financial capital (you sell your labor, save the proceeds), so it shrinks toward zero by retirement.

Why it reshapes risk-taking: if your large human-capital "asset" is bond-like and stable (a tenured professor, a civil servant), your total balance sheet is already heavily "in bonds," so your financial portfolio can hold more equity early on; if your human capital is equity-like and volatile (a commission trader, a startup founder, someone whose income tracks the market), your financial portfolio should hold less. The portfolio's job is to balance the whole balance sheet, not just the investable part.

The lifecycle implication (G165): young → human-capital-rich and financial-capital-poor → take more financial risk and save aggressively; old → human-capital-poor → de-risk, because there is no longer a long earnings stream to recover from a market loss. This is the theoretical engine under the glide path (G164) and target-date funds.

The connection to risk capacity (G163): a big slug of safe human capital raises a household's capacity to bear portfolio risk; as human capital depletes, capacity falls — which is why a sensible glide path de-risks with age.

The frontier and the simplification Berns makes: ideally the volatility and market-correlation of human capital would drive the allocation directly; in practice most planning (and Berns's own model) treats human capital as bond-like and instead anchors on whether the household is saving enough to fund its goals (standard-of-living risk / discretionary wealth, Z1.6).
connects_to_raw: `G162` (this node), `G163` risk capacity, `G164` glide path, `G165` lifecycle investing, `G169` decumulation (human capital ≈ 0 by here), `★ G143` asset allocation (human capital is the hidden asset class), `★ G146` diversification (job + portfolio should diversify each other — don't load the 401(k) with employer stock), Z2.10 (target-date funds), AM Z2.10 (the institutional life-cycle/glide-path note). **GAP: human-capital modeling depth (volatility, correlation, labor flexibility) — Berns flags this as a simplification.**

gaps.note: human-capital modeling depth (volatility, correlation, labor flexibility) — Berns flags this as a simplification

title: Risk Capacity vs. Risk Tolerance
quick_definition: Two different things the industry constantly conflates: risk tolerance is how much volatility a client can emotionally stomach (a psychological preference); risk capacity is how much risk their goals and balance sheet can actually withstand (a financial constraint) — and a prudent portfolio is governed by the lower of the two.
explainer_covers: The distinction (Berns's core idea): tolerance is subjective (a feeling, measurable but irrational — Berns measures it with three lottery-style questionnaires for risk aversion, loss aversion, and reflection); capacity is objective (set by the goals, the time horizon, the savings rate, and the balance sheet). The two are independent and frequently in conflict.

Standard-of-living risk and discretionary wealth (Berns's engine): capacity is made concrete through discretionary wealth (assets minus the present value of the liabilities/goals) and standard-of-living risk (SLR, an inverse measure of the probability of meeting goals). When SLR is high (goals barely funded), risk aversion must be moderated upward — the client can't afford to take the risk they might emotionally want; when SLR is low (goals over-funded), the client's natural risk tolerance can govern.

The "use the lower of the two" rule: you never run a portfolio at a risk level above the client's capacity, no matter how high their tolerance (a thrill-seeker near a hard goal must still de-risk); and you generally don't run far below capacity if that would fail to fund the goals (an over-cautious client with under-saved goals may need more equity, paired with coaching). Berns frames this as deploying the higher of subjective and objective risk aversion.

Time-varying tolerance: risk tolerance itself drifts — clients feel more risk-averse after losses (the "break-even effect") and less after gains (the "house-money effect") — so the advisor should measure it periodically and anchor on the average-environment level, a core behavioral-coaching task (G161).

Why this is the heart of suitability: matching the portfolio to capacity (not just stated tolerance) is the substance of acting in the client's best interest (G181) — and the most common planning error is letting a client's high tolerance override their low capacity, or vice versa.
connects_to_raw: `G163` (this node), `G162` human capital (raises capacity), `G160` goals-based investing (each goal sets a capacity), `G164` glide path (capacity falls with horizon), `G161` behavioral coaching (managing time-varying tolerance), `G181` fiduciary vs. suitability (matching to capacity *is* the best-interest test), `★ G116` drawdown/volatility (what tolerance is a tolerance *for*), Z3.2 (allocation under capacity). *Real-world layer: Berns Ch. 2 (the client risk profile; SLR; discretionary wealth).*
real_world_layer: Berns Ch. 2 (the client risk profile; SLR; discretionary wealth).

title: Tax-Advantaged Accounts: The Wrappers
quick_definition: The account types that shelter investment growth from tax — tax-deferred and tax-free retirement accounts plus targeted vehicles for education and health — whose contribution limits, tax treatment, and withdrawal rules frame the entire household saving-and-decumulation plan.
explainer_covers: The two big tax treatments: tax-deferred (traditional IRA / 401(k): contributions may be pre-tax, growth is untaxed, withdrawals are taxed as ordinary income) vs. tax-free (Roth IRA / Roth 401(k): contributions are after-tax, growth and qualified withdrawals are tax-free) — the central "pay tax now or later?" decision, driven by whether you expect a higher tax rate now or in retirement.

The account menagerie (Swensen's "alphanumeric soup"): IRAs, 401(k)/403(b)/457 employer plans, SEP and SIMPLE IRAs for the self-employed, 529 education-savings accounts (tax-free for qualified education), and HSAs (the triple-tax-advantaged health account) — each with its own limits, rules, and ideal use.

Why the wrapper matters as much as the investment: sheltering high-tax assets inside these accounts is the single most powerful lever an individual has to close the gap between pre-tax and after-tax return (Swensen: taxes can cost a taxable equity investor multiple percentage points a year) — which is why asset location (G166) decides what goes in which wrapper.

The contribution-and-withdrawal rules that shape the plan: annual contribution limits, early-withdrawal penalties, required minimum distributions (RMDs) that force taxable withdrawals from traditional accounts in later life, and the Roth's absence of RMDs — all of which drive both the accumulation plan and the decumulation order (Z4).

The estate angle: retirement accounts pass by beneficiary designation (outside the will), and inherited-account rules (the "see-through trust," the 10-year payout) make them a distinct estate-planning problem (G173).
connects_to_raw: `G168` (this node), `G166` tax-aware investing & asset location, `G167` tax-loss harvesting (a *taxable*-account technique — the wrappers are where you *don't* need it), `G169` decumulation (withdrawal order across wrappers), `G159` financial planning, `G173` estate planning (beneficiary designations, inherited accounts), Z4.1 (decumulation sequencing), AM Z1.7 (the institution's tax-exemption — *the wrapper an endowment gets for free*). **GAP: a current tax-code/account-rules reference — limits and rules change yearly and are jurisdiction-specific (US-centric here).**

gaps.note: a current tax-code/account-rules reference — limits and rules change yearly and are jurisdiction-specific (US-centric here

title: How Advisors Get Paid: Fee Models
quick_definition: Advice is paid for in a few distinct ways — a percentage of assets under management, flat or hourly fee-only planning, commissions on products sold, or a fee-based hybrid — and each model embeds different incentives and conflicts the client must understand to know whose interest is being served.
explainer_covers: The AUM model (dominant): the advisor charges an annual percentage of assets managed (often ~1%, declining for larger accounts) — aligning the advisor with growing the portfolio, but charging the same whether or not advice adds value, and creating conflicts around advice that reduces AUM (paying off a mortgage, buying an annuity, gifting to heirs).

Fee-only / flat / hourly planning: the advisor is paid directly by the client for advice (a retainer, a project fee, an hourly rate) and sells no products — the model with the fewest product conflicts, and the one most cleanly aligned with planning (G159) rather than asset-gathering.

Commission: the advisor (historically a broker) is paid by the product provider when the client buys a fund, annuity, or insurance policy — the model with the sharpest conflict, since it rewards selling the highest-commission product rather than the best one (the abuse Swensen documents in the for-profit fund industry).
Fee-based (hybrid): a mix — an AUM or planning fee plus commissions on some products — common and legitimate but requiring disclosure, because the conflicts of both models coexist.

Why the model is inseparable from the fiduciary question (G181): how someone is paid shapes whose interest they serve — which is exactly why the fiduciary-vs-suitability distinction (Z1.9) matters most where commissions and product sales are involved. The cost-drag arithmetic (★ G150) applies to advice fees too: a 1% AUM fee, compounded over decades, is a large share of lifetime return — so the fee must buy commensurate planning and behavioral value (G161).
connects_to_raw: `G180` (this node), `G181` fiduciary vs. suitability, `G161` the advisor's value (what the fee must buy), `★ G150` cost/fee drag (advice fees compound too), `★ G151` AUM economics (the advisor's business is an AUM business), `★ G8` management fee (the *product* fee layered under the advice fee — the "fee on a fee" problem), `★ G158` the active-vs-passive debate (cheap products make the advice fee more visible), Z5.1 (the WM firm as a business). *Real-world layer: Swensen,* Unconventional Success *(the for-profit-fund conflict).*

title: Fiduciary vs. Suitability
quick_definition: The defining standard-of-care question in advice: a fiduciary must act in the client's best interest and put the client first; a (historically lower) suitability standard required only that a recommendation be "suitable" — a distinction that decides whose interest wins when advisor and client incentives diverge.
explainer_covers: The two standards: a fiduciary (a registered investment adviser, a true financial planner) owes duties of loyalty and care — act solely in the client's interest, disclose and manage conflicts, control costs; a suitability standard (historically the broker-dealer world) required only that a product fit the client's situation, even if a cheaper or better one existed and even if the broker earned more on the one sold.

Why it matters in dollars: under suitability, a broker could sell a high-commission, high-fee fund that was "suitable" but inferior — the precise mechanism by which, in Swensen's account, the for-profit fund industry extracted wealth from individuals. The fiduciary standard is meant to close that gap.

The same duty, two seats (the cross-module link): this is the individual-facing version of the fiduciary duty an asset manager owes its fund (★ G155) and a GP owes its LPs (PE Z1.20) — the same legal and ethical concept, here owed by an advisor to a person rather than by a manager to a pool.

The regulatory patchwork and its motion: the standard varies by who the advisor is (RIA vs. broker-dealer), by jurisdiction, and over time (the US has moved toward — and partly back from — a uniform best-interest standard; Reg BI; the on-again/off-again fiduciary rules for retirement advice) — a live, contested area.
The practical client takeaway: ask how the advisor is paid (G180) and what standard they're held to — the two questions that, together, reveal whose interest comes first.
connects_to_raw: `G181` (this node), `G180` fee models, `★ G155` fiduciary duty (the institutional/GP version — *the same duty*), `G161` the advisor relationship, `★ G150` cost/fee drag (the cost the duty obliges controlling), Z4.4 (acting in the client's interest through life events), **PE Z1.20** (the GP's fiduciary duty to LPs), **AM Z1.7** (the manager's fiduciary duty — *the same node, fund seat*). **GAP: jurisdiction- and regulation-specific advice law (Reg BI, the DOL fiduciary saga, the global patchwork) — recency.**

gaps.note: jurisdiction- and regulation-specific advice law (Reg BI, the DOL fiduciary saga, the global patchwork) — recency

title: Where Wealth Management Sits Among the Modules
quick_definition: The map of where wealth management touches the five sibling modules — as the individual-client counterpart to Asset Management, the retail (and HNW) demand for the products every other module builds, the place the institutional allocator's seat gets a private-client mirror, and the channel through which households reach public markets, funds, and (for the wealthy) alternatives.
explainer_covers: WM ↔ Asset Management: the two halves of the money-management world (Z1.2) — same products and analytics, split by individual-vs-institutional objective; wealth management is also AM's distribution channel (AM Z5.3) and the buyer of its funds. The densest seam in this module.

WM ↔ the institutional allocator (the LP-side mirror): the endowment/pension/foundation seat (AM Z5.4, ★ G154, and the LP-side nodes of PE Z5, HF Z5, Credit Z5) has a private-client mirror — the family office (G177) and the HNW investor who, like a mini-endowment, sets a policy portfolio, selects managers, and (uniquely) plans around taxes, a finite horizon, and an estate. The endowment model is the contrast that defines the individual's problem.

WM ↔ Investment Banking: wealth-management clients are often created by IB events — the founder whose company is sold or taken public (★ G102), the executive with concentrated equity comp — and the resulting liquidity event is where concentrated-stock management and the whole planning apparatus engage; the wealthy also buy the IPOs and bond deals IB underwrites.

WM ↔ Private Equity, Private Credit & Hedge Funds: for HNW/UHNW clients, wealth management is the retail-and-private-wealth demand for alternatives — the PE, private-credit, and hedge-fund products (★ G9, ★ G56, ★ G57) that the democratization/evergreen-vehicle trend (PE Z5.22, AM Z5.6) is pushing down into the wealth channel; the individual is the ultimate LP (★ G7), reaching these funds through advisors, feeders, and now semi-liquid vehicles.

The unifying lesson: one capital market, now six seats — the bank advises and finances, PE owns private companies, private credit lends and holds, the hedge fund trades for absolute return, the asset manager holds for a benchmark at scale, and the wealth manager plans, allocates, and coaches a family's money across a human life — assembling the products of all five into one household's goals.
connects_to_raw: `G160` goals-based investing, `G177` the family office, `★ G154` endowment model (the allocator mirror), `★ G102` the IPO process (the client-creating event), `★ G7` LP (the individual as ultimate LP), `★ G9` "2 and 20" (alternatives in the wealth channel), **AM Z1.9** (the asset-management side of this map — *where AM names WM*), **AM Z5.4** (the institutional allocator the family office mirrors), **PE Z5.22** (retail democratization of PE), **AM Z5.6** (the wealth-channel push). *This is the module's "how it all connects" hub — build it as the WM counterpart to AM Z1.9.* ---
